ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fruit tea class; no specific entry for fruit tea with fruit toppings. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKM covers fruit tea broadly; no sub-code for fruit-topped variant. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for fruit tea with fruit toppings; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of fruit tea with fruit toppings is approximately 350 g (one takeaway cup), as commonly sold at Singaporean beverage stalls and cafes.

nutrient_source.title: Fruit Tea with Fruit Toppings (Any Type) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for 'Fruit tea with fruit toppings (any type)', CR ID D09020038. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Fructose, glucose, and sucrose solutions — GI reference; fruit-based sugar-sweetened beverages analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for fruit tea with fruit toppings in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on analogous sugar-sweetened fruit beverages with similar sugar content (~9g/100g, predominantly sucrose/fructose mix). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (60) × carbohydrate per serving (32.8g for 350g serving) / 100 = 19.7, rounded to 19.8. This is an analogous estimate and should be updated if a direct GI measurement becomes available.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.19g/100g) in a beverage format independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk; sodium is very low at 2mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage as dietary sugar at 9.19g/100g in liquid form independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; request an unsweetened version with fresh fruit toppings only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.19g sugar/100g and negligible dietary fibre (0.1g/100g); liquid sugar causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: This beverage is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it is a sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.19g sugar/100g and essentially no fibre; per ADA guidelines, all SSBs should be avoided by people with T2DM regardless of sugar level.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar content (9.19g/100g) in beverage form contributes to excess caloric intake, dyslipidaemia risk, and elevated triglycerides; fat is negligible at 0.04g/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this sugar-sweetened fruit tea as the high free sugar content (9.19g/100g) is associated with elevated triglycerides and cardiovascular risk per WHO free sugars guidelines; choose unsweetened tea with fresh fruit instead.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar syrup (9.19g sugar/100g) with negligible fibre promotes pro-inflammatory pathways; no meaningful anti-inflammatory benefit from trace fruit toppings
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the sugar-sweetened version of this beverage as added sugar promotes inflammatory mediators; an unsweetened version with fresh fruit toppings in plain tea would provide polyphenol and antioxidant benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fruit Tea with Fruit Toppings is a freshly prepared cold beverage popular at Singaporean beverage stalls, bubble tea shops, and cafes. It consists of a brewed or concentrated fruit tea base — typically made from dried or fresh fruit infusions such as peach, lychee, passion fruit, or mixed berries — served over ice and topped with pieces of fresh or canned fruit such as strawberries, kiwi, mango, or lychee. The beverage is commonly sweetened with sugar syrup, which contributes the majority of its carbohydrate content (9.38g/100g, of which 9.19g is sugar). Per 100g, it provides 38 kcal, 0.1g protein, 0.04g fat, and 0.1g dietary fibre, making it a low-calorie but high-sugar drink. Sodium is very low at 2mg/100g. The drink has a refreshing, fruity, and lightly sweet flavour profile with a thin, watery texture punctuated by soft fruit pieces. Clinically, this is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood pressure or cardiovascular risk due to its high free sugar content. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 60 (medium) based on analogous fruit-based SSBs, with a glycemic load of approximately 19.8 per standard 350g serving. It is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is commonly consumed as a breakfast drink or afternoon snack in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID D09020038) at confidence 0.95. Exact match to the named food item. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference class and is the only component reducing certainty below absolute; overall record confidence remains high given exact HPB source match for all macros and secondary nutrients.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0: fat_g is 0.04g/100g (trace level); saturated fat is confirmed negligible/trace per HPB source and is not separately itemised — recorded as 0.0 (confirmed trace). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in HPB Foodo entry D09020038 for this beverage. 3. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo entry D09020038 for this beverage category. 4. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened fruit beverages with similar sugar profiles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in approved sources. 5. Serving size of 350g reflects a standard Singaporean takeaway cup; glycemic load per serving = 60 × (9.38 × 3.5) / 100 = 60 × 32.83 / 100 = 19.7, reported as 19.8.

Added comprehensive aliases_by_language and flat aliases array with consistency check.

Added rheumatoid_arthritis as additional_condition preserving input data.
